2605.23819.title: Not Too Generative, Not Too Discriminative: The Human Alignment Sweet Spot
2605.23819.paper_summary: A central question in computational vision is whether human-like visual representations are better explained by discriminative or generative learning. Existing comparisons, however, often confound the learning objective with architecture, scale, and training data, leaving open whether the objective itself drives alignment. We address this confound using Joint Energy-Based Models (JEMs), which interpolate continuously between discriminative and generative training within a fixed architecture. By varying a single mixing coefficient, we isolate the effect of the learning objective and evaluate the resulting models across six human-alignment benchmarks spanning perceptual similarity, gloss perception, human response uncertainty, robustness, shape-texture cue conflict, and diagnostic feature attribution. Across this diverse suite, human alignment is consistently maximized at intermediate points of the generative-discriminative continuum, rather than at either endpoint. Hybrid JEMs combine the categorical structure induced by discriminative learning with the sensitivity to input structure induced by generative learning, yielding more human-like behavior across multiple levels of vision. These results suggest that the generative-discriminative dichotomy is the wrong axis for understanding human-aligned vision: alignment emerges not from choosing one objective over the other, but from balancing both.
2605.23819.paper_summary_zh: 一個計算視覺中的核心問題是，人類般的視覺表徵是否更好地由判別學習或生成學習來解釋。然而，現有的比較往往將學習目標與架構、規模和訓練數據混淆，並未解決目標本身是否驅動對齊。我們使用聯合能量基模型（JEMs）來解決這一混淆，該模型在固定架構內持續插值於判別訓練和生成訓練之間。通過變化單一的混合係數，我們孤立出學習目標的影響，並在六個涵蓋感知相似性、光澤感知、人類反應不確定性、穩健性、形狀-紋理線索衝突和診斷特徵歸因的人類對齊基準上評估結果模型。在這一多樣化的套件中，人類對齊在生成-判別連續體的中間點上始終達到最大化，而不是在任一端點。混合 JEMs 結合了判別學習所誘導的類別結構與生成學習所誘導的對輸入結構的敏感性，從而在多個視覺層面上產生更類似人類的行為。這些結果表明，生成-判別二分法並不是理解人類對齊視覺的正確軸心：對齊不是通過選擇一個目標而非另一個目標來產生的，而是通過平衡兩者來實現的。
2605.23819.author: Jorge Chang Ortega et.al.
2605.23819.authors: Jorge Chang Ortega, Bastien Le Lan, Thomas Serre, Victor Boutin
